response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion:
Look, I'm not someone who spends all day scrolling through social media like some people do. I check in a few times a week, mostly to see what's happening with friends and family, maybe some travel inspiration for my next trip outside Bologna. But even with my limited time online, I've noticed how much nonsense gets shared around, and honestly, it bothers me. When you work in a salon all day talking to different people, you hear about the wild things they believe they saw online, and half the time it's completely fabricated.

I've learned that you really need to be skeptical about what you see. Just because something has a lot of likes or shares doesn't mean it's true. I make a point now of checking the source before I believe anything, especially health claims or beauty advice that pops up on my feed. With my job, I get asked about skincare routines and treatments all the time, and I've had to correct so many myths that started from fake before-and-after photos.

What works for me is keeping things simple. I don't get caught up in every trend or dramatic story. If something seems too outrageous or too perfect, I ask myself whether I've heard about it from reliable sources. My son is growing up with all this technology, so I'm careful about what he sees too. I want him to understand that not everything online is real.

I think people need to be more cautious and less trusting of what algorithms feed them. It's not enough to be passive about it. Take a moment to verify things, especially before you share them with others. In my salon, I see how misinformation spreads through conversations, and it starts from someone trusting something they saw online without questioning it. We all have a responsibility to be more thoughtful. It's not complicated, just requires paying attention and using some common sense.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 40-year-old hairdresser from Bologna with moderate conscientiousness and extraversion but low openness and agreeableness. Her curiosity drives engagement with the topic, while her moderate conscientiousness suggests practical, systematic thinking about verification. Her low openness indicates preference for established, straightforward approaches rather than experimental methods. She spends limited time on social media (few times weekly), suggesting measured engagement. Her work environment exposes her to how misinformation spreads through word-of-mouth. Her single parenthood and professional responsibility inform her perspective on protecting her son and providing accurate information to clients. The tone reflects someone pragmatic, slightly skeptical, and grounded in real-world consequences of fake content.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, practical, slightly skeptical, grounded in real-world experience, maternal and protective, conversational but authoritative within her domain of expertise
citations: Personal professional experience in beauty industry
